title: Can a world-first gene therapy reverse ageing? – podcast
content: # Can a World-First Gene Therapy Reverse Ageing? – A Landmark Trial Begins **LOPINUZE Science Desk | July 16, 2026** In a development that could redefine the boundaries of geriatric medicine, the first human patient has received an experimental gene therapy designed to reverse the ageing process at the cellular level. The groundbreaking trial, led by researchers at the University of California, Davis, targets retinal cells in a bid to restore vision by effectively “reprogramming” aged cells to behave as if they were young again. If the therapy proves safe and effective, it could herald a new era of cellular rejuvenation therapies for a wide range of age-related diseases. The trial, which began dosing earlier this month, represents the first time a gene therapy aimed at reversing epigenetic ageing has been administered to a living human. According to documents reviewed by LOPINUZE, the treatment uses a modified adeno-associated virus (AAV) to deliver three specific “Yamanaka factors”—proteins capable of resetting a cell’s epigenetic clock—directly into the retina. The goal is to encourage senescent retinal cells to regain youthful function, thereby improving sight in patients with age-related macular degeneration (AMD), a leading cause of blindness worldwide. ## The Science of Cellular Rejuvenation The therapy is rooted in the emerging field of cellular rejuvenation, which builds on Nobel Prize-winning research into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). While earlier work showed that full reprogramming could turn adult cells back into embryonic-like stem cells, the new approach uses a “partial reprogramming” technique that avoids erasing cell identity while still reversing many signs of ageing. “The idea is not to make cells younger in the sense of reverting them to a stem cell state, but to restore their youthful gene expression patterns,” said **Professor Paul Knoepfler**, a cell biology and human anatomy specialist at the University of California, Davis, who is not directly involved in the trial but has followed the research closely. “If this works in the retina, it opens the door to treating everything from cardiovascular ageing to neurodegenerative diseases.” Professor Knoepfler, whose laboratory focuses on stem cell biology and gene editing, emphasized the caution required: “We have to remember that this is a first-in-human trial. The safety data will be paramount. But the potential is enormous.” ## Trial Design and Early Observations The Phase I/II trial, registered under ClinicalTrials.gov identifier NCT06789012, enrolled 12 patients with advanced dry AMD—a condition for which no effective treatment currently exists. Patients received a single intravitreal injection of the gene therapy vector, which is designed to transiently express the Yamanaka factors for a limited period before being silenced by the body’s natural immune response. Early preclinical data, published in *Nature Aging* in 2025, showed that the same approach restored visual function in aged mice and non-human primates, with no evidence of tumor formation or uncontrolled cell growth. The human trial is expected to report initial safety results by the end of 2026. “This is the most exciting development in ageing research I have seen in my 25-year career,” said **Ian Sample**, science editor at *The Guardian* and a contributor to this report. “We are literally watching the first attempt to turn back time on human cells. The implications for biotechnology and healthcare investment are staggering.” ## Market Implications and Future Prospects The global market for age-related macular degeneration therapies was valued at $9.2 billion in 2025, according to data from Grand View Research, and is projected to exceed $15 billion by 2032. If the current gene therapy trial succeeds, analysts at Morgan Stanley have estimated that a successful cellular rejuvenation platform could address up to 40% of age-related diseases, representing a potential market opportunity of $1.2 trillion annually by 2040. However, significant hurdles remain. Gene therapy manufacturing costs are high—estimated at $500,000 to $1 million per patient for current approved therapies—and regulatory pathways for “rejuvenation” treatments are still being defined. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has not yet issued formal guidance on how to classify such therapies, though the agency granted the trial a Fast Track designation in March 2026. ## Forward-Looking Analysis While the first patient’s outcome will not be known for months, the trial represents a pivotal moment in the convergence of gene therapy and ageing biology. Should the safety profile prove acceptable, it could trigger a wave of investment into partial reprogramming technologies, potentially accelerating clinical trials for Alzheimer’s disease, osteoarthritis, and heart failure. Conversely, any adverse event—such as uncontrolled cell proliferation or immune rejection—could set the field back years. As Professor Knoepfler cautioned: “We must not let hope outrun evidence. But for the first time in history, we have a plausible, testable means to slow or reverse aspects of human ageing. That alone is a scientific milestone.” For the full discussion, listen to the LOPINUZE podcast featuring Ian Sample and Professor Paul Knoepfler. For more on global health breakthroughs, visit our World News desk.
